18.display: Yes — they are always equal (an identity).

23.display: Priya is wrong — an identity must be true for every value of x. 2x + 3 = 7 is only true for x = 2, so it is an equation with a single solution, not an identity.

24.display: Always — expanding the left-hand side gives 3x + 6, which matches the right-hand side for every value of x.

25.display: Sam is wrong — x + x = 2x is true for every value of x, not just x = 0. It is an identity, not an equation with a single solution.

26.display: 2x + 4 = 10 is true at x = 3 (its only solution) but false at x = 5 — it is an equation. 2(x + 2) ≡ 2x + 4 is true at both x = 3 and x = 5 (and every value) — it is an identity.

27.display: True — an identity holds for every possible value of x (infinitely many), while a linear equation like this usually has exactly one solution.

28.display: Leo is wrong — x^2 = 4 is not true for every number, e.g. it fails at x = 3. It is an equation with two solutions, x = 2 and x = -2, not an identity.

29.display: 4(x + 1) = 4x + 4 is an identity — expanding the left side always gives 4x + 4. 4(x + 1) = 20 is an equation with one solution, x = 4.

30.display: Since the statement is true for some values (x = 4) and false for others (x = 1), it is an equation with a specific solution, not an identity.

31.display: At x = 4: 2(12) = 24 = 6(4). At x = -2: 2(-6) = -12 = 6(-2). Both true, so 2(3x) = 6x is an identity, true for every number.

32.display: Testing a few numbers can only show the formula worked for those cases; there could still be a value where it fails that wasn't tried. Only expanding and simplifying algebraically can prove it holds for every possible value.

33.display: 2s - s ≡ s. Check with s = 6: 2(6) - 6 = 12 - 6 = 6, which matches s = 6.

34.display: It is an identity, not an equation — addition is commutative, so x + 5 equals 5 + x for every value of x; there is no single value to solve for.

35.display: At x = 2: (x+1)^2 = 9 but x^2 + 1 = 5. They are not equal, so this is not an identity — it is disproved by this counterexample.

36.display: (p/2) × 2 ≡ p. Check with p = 14: (14/2) × 2 = 7 × 2 = 14, which matches p = 14.

37.display: 3(x + 4) - 2x = 3x + 12 - 2x = x + 12, which matches the right-hand side, confirming the identity.

38.display: 2(x - 1) + 2(x + 1) = 2x - 2 + 2x + 2 = 4x, which matches the right-hand side, confirming the identity.

39.display: Not an identity. At x = 1: (x+2)^2 = 9 but x^2 + 4 = 5. At x = 3: (x+2)^2 = 25 but x^2 + 4 = 13. Both tests disprove the identity.

40.display: Example identity: 2(x + 3) ≡ 2x + 6 (true for all x). Example equation: 2x + 6 = 10 (true only when x = 2). Any correctly proven pair is acceptable.

41.display: The first has an equals sign and can be solved; the second is just an expression

42.display: Yes — both sides give 16 when x = 3
